California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

August 19, 1988

William R. McKown
Acting City Administrator
City of Carson
P.O. Box 6234
Carson, CA 90749

Re: Your Request For Advice
Our File No. A-88-276

Dear Mr. McKown:

You have requested advice about application of Section
89001 of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")1/ as amended by
recently enacted Proposition 73, to the City of Carson monthly
report.

QUESTIONS

You asked the following questions about the January and
June 1988 copies of the City of Carson monthly report entitled
"The Carson Report."

1. May the city publish portrait photographs of its
elected officials in the monthly report?

2. May the city publish group photographs of the city's
elected officials in the report, if the officials are not
identified by name?

3. May the city publish a feature article introducing a
newly appointed councilmember?

_____________________

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise
indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code
of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to
regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code
of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

William R. McKown
August 19, 1988
Page -2-

     4.  Is there a difference between a report being mailed at
public expense and being delivered at public expense?

                         CONCLUSIONS

     The city may not publish photographs of or otherwise refer
to elected officers in the city's monthly report or in any
other mass mailing.  This prohibition includes publishing group
photographs in which elected city officers are not identified
by name.  Furthermore, for purposes of the Act a person
appointed to a vacant elective office is an elected officer.
Therefore, the city may not publish a feature article about a
newly appointed councilmember.

     Nevertheless, the city may list the names of elected
officers in a standard roster-type listing, as long as names of
all elected officers are in the same type size and typeface.

     The Commission also has concluded that a public agency may
not send a mass mailing in any way that results in expenditure
of public moneys.  Delivering a document is a form of sending a
document.  Therefore, the city may not spend public moneys to
deliver mass mailings that contain the name, signature, or
photograph of an elected officer.

                            FACTS

     The city of Carson publishes a monthly report entitled "The
Carson Report."  On the address page of the January 1988
report, the city published captioned portrait photographs of
the city's seven elected officers: five councilmembers, the
city clerk and city treasurer.

     The June 1988 report had several references to elected city
officers.  That issue's front-page feature article introduced a
newly appointed member of the city council; the article
included a large portrait photograph of the official.  Inside
the report, three different group photographs showed several
councilmembers, who were identified by name in the captions.
Finally, the address page had captioned portrait photographs of
the city's seven elected officers.

                           ANALYSIS

     California electors enacted Proposition 73 in June 1988.
Proposition 73 includes amendments to Sections 89001 and

William R. McKown
August 19, 1988
Page -3-

82041.5 of the Act.  These amendments took effect on June 8,
1988.

     Section 89001 now reads:

          89001.  No newsletter or other mass mailing shall
     be sent at public expense.

     Section 82041.5 now reads:

          82041.5.  "Mass mailing" means two hundred or
     more substantially similar pieces of mail, but does
     not include a form letter or other mail which is sent
     in response to an unsolicited request, letter or other
     inquiry.

     These two sections could be construed to ban all
mailings at public expense of 200 or more substantially
similar documents.  On July 26, 1988, however, the
Commission adopted an emergency regulation and approved
interim advice that allows public agencies to send certain
mass mailings at public expense.  (Regulation 18901; Raye
Advice Letters, No. A-88-220, copies enclosed.)  In the
following paragraphs we apply emergency Regulation 18901
and the interim advice letters to the monthly reports
published by the City of Carson.

Prohibition Against Spending Public Moneys To Promote Elected
Officials

     After considering ballot arguments in favor of Proposition
73, statements by the proponents of the proposition, and the
language of amended Section 89001, the Commission concluded
that the intent of Section 89001 is to prohibit the use of
public moneys for the purpose of sending out mass mailings
which assist elected officials in the quest for public office.
(Raye Advice Letter, No. A-88-220, June 16, 1988.)  Therefore,
any reference to an elected officer is prohibited in a public
agency newsletter sent to 200 or more persons in a calendar
month, except in the standard letterhead or in a roster-type
listing in which each elected officer's name appears in the
same type size and typeface as the other officers.  (See
emergency Regulation 18901; Raye Advice Letter, No. A-88-220,
July 1, 1988.)

     Consequently, the city may not publish portrait or group
photographs of elected officers in the monthly report, even if
the captions do not identify the officers by name.  Also,
Section 82020 defines an elected officer to include "a person
who is appointed to fill a vacant elective office....."  Thus, a

William R. McKown
August 19, 1988
Page -4-


newly appointed councilmember is an elected officer for
purposes of the Act. Therefore, the city cannot publish and
send a feature article about the newly appointed councilmember
or use a photograph of the officer. (Also see Gonzalez Advice
Letter, No. A-88-021, copy enclosed.) The city however, may
list its elected officers in the standard letterhead or in a
roster-type listing that uses the same type size and typeface
for each officer.

Sending A Mass Mailing

     For the meantime, the Commission has concluded that Section
89001 prohibits a public agency from spending public moneys to
distribute mass mailings that contain the names or photographs
of elected officers. (Raye Advice Letter, No. A-88-220, July
1, 1988, at page 5.) The terms "sent" and "mailing" include
delivering documents. (Raye Advice Letter, No. A-88-220, July
1, 1988, at page 6, footnote 4.) Therefore, the city may not
use public moneys to pay for any type of delivery service to
distribute a mass mailing containing the name or photograph of
an elected officer.

     I hope this letter provides satisfactory answers to your
advice request. Please call me at (916) 322-5901 if you have
any questions about this letter.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Diane M. Griffiths
                                        General Counsel
                                        
                                        [signature]
                                        By: Margarita Altamirano
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

DMG:MA:aa

Enclosures

CITY OF CARSON

July 12, 1988

Fair Political Practice Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95814

Attention: Legal Division

Please find attached two copies of Monthly Reports for the City of Carson,
California. Each copy is marked in yellow as items to be deleted per our
understanding of your staff's interpretation of newly enacted Proposition
73. I would appreciate specific comments on each report as to the following:

1.      Does the yellow marked deletions bring the Report within
        the FPPC Guidelines.

2.      Are group pictures such as those in Report #2 dated June
        1988 acceptable if Council names are deleted?

3.      Can a one time feature article introducing a newly appointed
        Councilmember be used as a Public Information piece.

4.      Is there a FPPC distinction made between mailed at public
        expense and delivered at public expense.

I would appreciate your immediate response so the City can modify our
Monthly Report to comply with the FPPC rules and still provide informative
reports to the City of Carson.

Sincerely,

William R. McKown
Acting City Administrator

Sincerely,

William R. McKown

CITY HALL • 701 EAST CARSON STREET • P.O. BOX 6234 • CARSON, CALIFORNIA 90749 • (213) 830-7600

THE
CARSON REPORT
THE CITY OF CARSON, CALIFORNIA

CARSON CITY HALL • 701 East Carson Street • Carson, CA 90745 • 830-7600 • JANUARY 1988

Carson City Government
Reorganization—1988

In an effort to provide the residents
and taxpayers a more efficient and
responsive City Government, the
Carson City Council approved a total
reorganization plan for city staff and
services on December 7, 1987.

The proposed changes will begin in
January, 1988, and will take approximately six to eight months to become
totally operational.

The City Council in this consolidated
effort desires to structure a City Government where citizen complaints and
efficient responses go hand-in-hand.
According to Councilmembers the
reorganization will assist each resident
in knowing who to contact if and when

they feel there is a need for public services. Along with the Reorganization
Chart, shown on the 2nd page of this
Carson Report, are the appropriate
names and telephone numbers of
Administrators who supervise the
activities.

This information will assist Carson
citizens who need public services or
information. Councilmembers felt that
it is important to give the Government
a general tune-up to make it more
responsive to your present day needs.
They hope you will keep this report
available to assist you in receiving
responsive services from Carson
City Hall.

City Council
Changes
Meeting Day
And Time

Beginning January 5, 1988, City
Council meetings will be held on the
first and third Tuesdays of every
month. Don't be late because the time
of the council meetings has also
changed and will be starting a half an
hour earlier, moving the meeting time
from 6:30 P.M. to 6:00 P.M., with the
Redevelopment Agency meeting following at 7:30 P.M.

The Carson Report • January 1988

Remodeling Of
City Hall's North
Wing Complete

Remodeling to hire damaged City Hall is
completed.

The Carson City Council has
announced that work has been completed on Carson City Hall's north
wing, which was heavily damaged, as a
result of a fire early in 1987. The
reconstruction of the north wing was
completed in eight months, and city
officials that repairs will begin on
the rest of the damaged City Hall
within 60 days.

North wing, which was remodeled
to resemble its pre-fire structure, was
open to bids for reconstruction. Damage estimates on the section, which
began at $500,000, ran well over
$1,000,000. Additionally, the rest of
City Hall must be repainted and damaged ceiling tiles replaced.

The fire, which caused over $1 million in total damage, was set by an
arsonist who broke in through a window in the north wing's Community
Development Department. The blaze
which caused not only the extensive
damage also suspended city operations
for one week.

An arsonists fire caused $1,000,000 in damage to the north wing of City Hall.

The Carson Report • January 1988

City Crowns
New Miss Carson

[IMAGE: Black and white photograph showing women wearing crowns]

Camille Tucker, a UCLA student, receives her crown after winning the recent Miss Carson pageant competition. Shown with her in the picture are (from left to right) Miss California Simone Stephens; Tucker, the outgoing Miss Carson, Bridget Avila Jones; and Cynthia Simpson, first runner-up. Not shown is Connie Howard, second runner-up and Kally Lewis, Miss Congeniality.

The new Miss Carson Camille Tucker, a UCLA student, received a $1,000 scholarship and will be making many public appearances as requested by the city of the chamber of commerce. She has also earned the right to compete for the Miss California title in San Diego on June 19, 1988.

The queen's court includes 1st runner up Cynthia Sympson, who received a $500 scholarship, and 2nd runner up Connie Howard, who received a $300 scholarship. In addition to receiving scholarships, the Queen and her court also received memberships to the IMB Academy Martial Arts and Fitness Center. Kally Lewis, who was chosen by the participants in the pageant to be Miss Congeniality, received a trophy and a $200 scholarship.

The Miss Carson Pageant, presented by the Carson Chamber of Commerce and the City of Carson, is the preliminary to the Miss California/Miss America Pageant (a scholarship pageant). This year's pageant was held at the Carson Community Center on November 22, 1987, when twelve young ladies competed for the title of Miss Carson.

Contestants were judged in four areas of competition, which included an interview (7½ minutes in duration), swim suit, and evening gown and talent competition.

The Miss Carson Pageant has been in existence since the incorporation of the City in 1968, but was not franchised with the Miss California/Miss America Pageant until July 1985.

The Pageant's purpose is to distribute money to young ladies wishing to further their education. This is achieved through the awarding of scholarships to not only the new queen and her court but to all of the participants for their involvement in the pageant. This year each girl received a $100 scholarship for her participation in the pageant.

Plaques of appreciation were awarded to corporate sponsors for their participation in the pageant. Among those present were John Moyer of the Shell Oil Company, Howard Duffy of the Caruso #438 Moose Lodge, and Richard S. Bustello of IMB Academy Martial Arts and Fitness Center. Also present was the President of the Miss California Pageant, Robert Arnhym.

Free Tax
Program For
Elderly And
Disabled

The Senior Social Services Department is offering assistance to Seniors and the disabled in filling out state and federal tax returns.

The tax counseling program is co-sponsored by the City of Carson, the Internal Revenue Service, and the Franchise Tax Board. Last year, the program helped an estimated 400 people in filing out state and federal tax returns.

The program is staffed by trained volunteers and is offered free of charge.

For futher information about the program please contact the Carson Senior Social Services at 801 E. Carson Street, or call 835-0212, ext. 203 or 208.

Council Recap

November 30, 1987
• Council approved a resolution adopting the City Budget and making appropriations for the 1987-88 fiscal year.

December 1, 1987
• Voted to support efforts to de-annex the northern most section of Carson from the Compton School District and annex it into the Los Angeles Unified School District.

• Voted to support the concept of a satellite municipal court in Carson and directed staff to study the matter and prepare a report for Council.

December 7, 1987
• Carson Redevelopment Agency voted to award a 6.2 million dollar contract for construction of Veterans Park. Project to begin in near future.

• Passed ordinance to establish zoning regulations for cargo container storage facilities.

The Carson Report • January 1988

The City Of Carson's Year In Review

1987 was an exciting and productive year for the City of Carson. Besides the individual accomplishments of so many city residents, City government produced a variety of services for the good of the community.

PARKS AND RECREATION had its usual busy year in 1987. Special events included the Annual Anniversary Celebration, Cultural Fair, Miss Carson Pageant, the Fine Arts Day Camp, The Carson Symphony, Los Caballeros Youth Band, Young Life Singers, Children's Playbox Theatre, the Tiny Tot programs (including the 1987 Mini-Olympics), and the many, many fine excursions to such places as Las Vegas, Tijuana and San Diego.

SENIORS had a wide range of programs from which to choose, such as the Dial-A-Ride program, the Friendly Visitor program, and one of 1987's highlights, Carson's hosting of the California Senior Legislature Candidates Forum and the election of Carson's first Senior Assemblyman.

Other SPECIAL EVENTS in Carson included the 4th of July Spectacular, Boxing Dinner Shows, Mardi Gras Celebration, Annual Country Western Fair, the Jazz Festival, Cinco de Mayo, Mexican Independence Day, Tribute to Dr. Martin Luther King, Winter Holiday Festival, Asian Pacific Islander Day, Culture in Black Festival, Halloween Carnivals at two park sites and the Samoan Athletes in Action/Youth Sports Clinic.

Gospel singers such as "The Nicholas Family" captioned will appear at Carson's 4th annual Martin Luther King Jr. Tribute, Jan. 16 from noon to 5 p.m. at Avalon Park.

Even though the stock market this year was highly volatile, reserves maintained by the City and Redevelopment Agency were invested wisely, resulting in substantial returns.

Reserves increased by nearly $15 million and an additional $4 million were transferred to the General Fund to pay for needed City services.

THE COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT DEPARTMENT, made up of three divisions, redevelopment, planning and building and safety responsibilities also reports accomplishments for the past year.

The department continued careful in-depth zoning studies related to industrial activities; sponsored the Arbor Day program, planting trees in City parks; sponsored an anti-litter program and promoted the annual Carson Beautiful program to recognize the homes and buildings which improve the beauty of the city.

REDEVELOPMENT is taking a more and more active role in the City. The new Carson Ibis Hotel and office complex broke ground in September of 1987. The project will consist of a seven story, 225-room hotel and 120,000 square foot office building adjacent to the Carson Community Building. The complex will result in additional revenues to the City and create over 200 new jobs.

THE VETERANS PARK site is in the final stages of cleanup. After the City discovered hazardous waste in the soil, they moved immediately to excavate and clean the soil. On December 7, 1987, the Council awarded a $6.2 million contract for construction of the park facility.

MAJOR CONSTRUCTION PROJECTS on City streets were completed during 1987, including Gardena Blvd., Wilmington, Main Street at 223rd Street, and curb, gutter and sidewalk improvements at 218th and Moneta. Also, Sepulveda Boulevard was rebuilt between the Harbor Freeway and Main Street, the largest public works project taken on by the City last year.

Sepulveda Blvd. reconstruction — from Harbor Freeway to Main St. — $835,000 project. Project included street resurfacing, landscaping of meridians, new street lighting and sidewalks.

THE SERVICE REQUEST DESK, which is the City's central locale for people with questions about services, handled over 1,850 calls from residents.

THE EMERGENCY SERVICES SECTION was the lead departments in preparing the new Multi-Hazard Functional Plan for this area of Los Angeles County, which is made up of 28 cities. After such an eventful year in 1987, one might expect a year of rest. But 1988 promises to be just as exciting a year for Carson!

Sheriff's Department Says Thanks

The Los Angeles County Sheriff's Department and the City of Carson would like to thank the many people who participated in their annual Family Assistance Program in December. Special thanks go to:

City employees who helped out after hours:
Joyce Cagaauan
Louis Lusero

Without the assistance of these many businesses and individuals, the program would not exist.

THE

CARSON REPORT
T H E C I T Y O F C A R S O N , C A L I F O R N I A

CARSON CITY HALL • 701 East Carson Street • Carson, CA 90745 • 830-7600 • June 1988

Carson's Newest Councilmember
John H. Anderson:
A True Civic Servant

His administrative experience
with the City of Carson includes
planning and supervising special
events, organizing numerous Senior
Citizen recreation programs and
serving on the Parks and Recreation Commission. As recognition
for his many years of public service,
the Councilman was also nominated for the John Anson Ford Award
for outstanding volunteer work
with senior citizens.

Councilman Anderson retired
from his 15-year career as a real
estate manager at Trust Properties
in 1981. He previously worked for
Security Pacific Bank and the federal government, but there was a
time when Anderson had to make a
difficult decision between playing
football with the New York Black
Bombers and using his college degree towards a conventional job.

Carson's newest Councilmember,
John Anderson, is no stranger to
serving the community: he's been
volunteering his athletic, musical
and social talents to Carson Seniors
for over 19 years.

A tennis pro, the 70-year old
Councilman gives lessons to Seniors through the recreation depart-

ment and he is said to give private
lessons "to any young person who
asks" at Bixby Knolls, his local
court.

Anderson is also an accomplished pianist. Of course, he has also
donated this special talent to the
Seniors of Carson, often delivering
performances to local Senior groups.

"At that time, I wanted a secure
job and a chance to use my education," he said.

But his athletic exploits have not
gone unnoticed. Councilman Anderson's alma mater, Benedict College in Columbia, South Carolina,
inducted him into its Athletic Hall
of Fame. Recalling his days as "the
O.J. Simpson of my class," Anderson stated, "One of the most valuable lessons I learned back then was
to take the ball and run over my
opponent, but to run around him...I
believe we should be students and
athletes all of our lives."

Councilman Anderson was appointed by the Carson City Council
to fill the unexpired term of Councilman Tom Mills. Councilman Mills
passed away last April.

Carson News • June 1988

Neighborhood Watch Expo
To Feature Protection Tips

Personal, home and business protection will be the topic of a free
Neighborhood Watch fair to be held
June 18 from 10 a.m. to 3 p.m. at the
Carson Community Center.
Sponsored by the Sheriff's Station and the City of Carson, the
event will include displays and
demonstrations on such topics as
missing children, bicycle safety and
licensing and safety for the elderly
and disabled. Tips about building
security, burglary prevention, false
alarms and home and business
security inspections will also be
offered.
The Sheriff's Department will have
displays and demonstrations of
some of its programs, such as the
SWAT (Special Weapons Team) and
K-9's (Police Dogs). Visitors will be
able to get a first-hand look at some
of the Sheriff and Fire Departments'

equipment, including a chance to
ride in the Fire Department's snorkel lift.
The "WeeTip" display will have information on drugs, toxic crime,
and crimes against children and
Seniors.
Free hot dogs, popcorn, soft
drinks will be served and special
prizes will be given away to promote
the safety programs.
Entertainment for the event will
include the Carson Clowners, the
Senior's band, the Young Life Singers, Los Caballeros Youth Band,
Polynesian fire dancers and ventriloquist act, Williams & Co.
The event will be held outside of
the Community Center which is
located at 801 E. Carson Street. For
more information call the Community Center at 835-0212.

Troubleshooter

Each month this section of the
newsletter will deal with a frequently asked question fielded by the staff
at the City's Service Request Desk
or will highlight important new information which might be of interest to the general public.

Question:

What noises are considered "unnecessary" under the City ordinance
and after what hour is it unlawful to
produce these noises?

Answer:

"Unnecessary noises are sounds
which are so harsh, prolonged or
physically annoying that they cause
discomfort to persons in the general area where the noise is being
made. Some noises that fall under
these specifications are stereos, televisions sets, band rehearsals, loudspeakers and amplifiers, engines
and motors, animals, construction
devices or simply yelling and
shouting.
In general, these noises are prohibited between the hours of 11
p.m. and 7 a.m. But some, like an
incessantly barking dog or person
shouting obscene language, are
always prohibited and may result in
a citation to the person responsible.
Some sounds, like that of advertising loudspeakers and construction
machinery, must cease by 9 p.m.

The Service Request Desk
is a clearing house for
any questions residents
may have regarding the
City of Carson.
If you are in need of a
particular service or would
like more information
regarding this issue,
please call
830-7600, ext. 320.

Local Runner Finishes 79th
In L.A. Marathon

Local Runner Martin Morino finished 79th out of more than 17,000 runners
participating in the recent Los Angeles Marathon. Morino is a student at CalState University, Dominguez Hills and is a management trainee at J.C. Penney's.
At a recent council meeting, Mr. Morino was honored for his outstanding
achievement. Pictured from left to right are: Front row, Mayor Kay Calas, Martin
Morino and Mayor Pro Tem Michael Mitoma. Back row, Councilwoman Sylvia
Muise and Councilwoman Vera Robles DeWitt.

Carson News • June 1988

City Seeks
A Few
Good People

APPLY NOW FOR SEATS
ON CITY COMMISSIONS
AND COMMITTEES

The City of Carson is now seeking citizens interested in becoming involved in local government as members of various commissions and committees.
Applications may be obtained at City Hall in the City Clerk's office.
All applications will be retained for consideration as vacancies occur during the year.
Service on commissions carries a $35 stipend, but those who sit on committees and boards do so on a volunteer basis. Carson residency and voter registration is required for membership on all commissions.
The following groups are seeking members:

COMMISSIONS:
• Planning
• Parks And Recreation
• Environmental
• Fine Arts And History
• Human Resources

COMMITTEES:
• Anniversary
• Citywide Advisory
• Economic Development
• Public Relations

BOARDS:
• Mobile Home Rent Review
• Relocation Appeals

More information may be obtained by contacting Helen Kawagoe at the City Clerk's office (830-7600).

Young Gymnast Chosen For
USA Junior National Team

At a recent meeting of the Carson City Council, gymnast Lily Garcia received a proclamation from the City in honor of her athletic achievement. Pictured from left to right are; Back row, Sylvia Muise, Councilwoman; John Anderson, Councilman and Vera Robles DeWitt, Councilwoman. Front row, Lily Garcia, gymnast and Michael Mitoma, Mayor Pro Tem.

Lily Garcia, an outstanding 13-year old gymnast from Carson, was recently chosen to compete on the USA Junior National Rhythmic Gymnastics Team.
Gymnasts for the 8-girl team were chosen at a grueling 2-day competition in Marin County. Lily is California State and Regional Hoop Champion. This will be her first international competition.
Lily trains for 4 hours a day, five days a week. Locally, she is on the West Coast Waves Team which is coached by Jolie Barretta and Sandi Cordey at Studio West in Palos Verdes. Also chosen from the Waves to compete with Lily, was Franca Abbatiello. Recently, Lily and Franca performed at the Compton Library.
Other performances for Lily include several cable T.V. programs, and the USA/USSR Exhibition at UCLA. According to Lily, performing at UCLA was particularly exciting because it gave her the opportunity to perform with top US and Soviet gymnasts, several of whom will be going to the Olympics. Lily, herself, is too young for the Olympics this year—but who knows about 1992?
Meanwhile Lily is concentrating on just having fun and getting the most out of gymnastics. "Rhythmic Gymnastics helps me feel creative and good about myself," she said. Her only goals for the moment are to have "high scores and clean routines," which should help her when she competes in the Nationals.

Carson News • June 1988

Fourth Of July
Spectacular
To Be Held
At Dominguez
Hills

A Fourth of July show sponsored
by the City of Carson will be held at
5 p.m. in the Velodrome at Cal State
Dominguez Hills.
The program will start off with
bicycle races and exhibitions by
various youth and professional organizations. The Carson-Dominguez
Hills Symphony orchestra led by
Dr. Frances Steiner will then perform from 8 p.m. through the duration of the fireworks show which
begins at approximately 9 p.m.
Free tickets and bus transportation will be provided by the City. For
tickets and bus schedules contact
City Hall or your neighborhood
park.

For more information call
Parks and Recreation at
830-7800, ext. 228.

Remodeling
Of City Hall
To Be
Completed in
Mid-August

Remodeling of Carson City Hall
should be completed by the middle
of August, according to Howard
Horena, Director of Parks and
Recreation.
The improvements include addition of a new South Wing, painting
inside and out, a new air-conditioning and electrical system, and an
updated security system.
The building's interior will have
new wall covering, new carpet and
new paint in both the council
chambers and offices.

Resident Receives
Medal Of Merit

Pictured from left to right are Front Row: Mayor Kay Calas, Jason Carroll and
Mayor Pro Tem Michael Mitoma. Back Row: Councilwoman Sylvia Muise and
Councilwoman Vera Robles DeWitt.

Only on rare occasions does the
City of Carson award its highest
tribute, The Medal of Merit. This
Medal is often awarded to people
who have endangered their own
lives to help others. Recently, The
Council presented this award to
Jason Carroll.
On March 15, Jason Carroll was
visiting at the Paradise Trailer
Lodge when a nearby mobile home
was engulfed in flames. Upon hearing a child's scream from a window in the home, Mr. Carroll smashed the window in an attempt to

rescue the child. The heat was too
intense, however. Mr. Carroll then
entered through a sliding door,
where he discovered an elderly
woman lying unconscious near the
entryway. As he carried the unconscious woman from the burning
home, the child jumped to safety
through the window broken by Carroll, earlier.
Through the heroic efforts of
Jason Carroll, Mrs. Concepcion
Bravo and her 2-year-old great
grandson were saved from a certain
death.

Free Job Training,
Placement Offered

Free job training and placement
is being offered by PIC works, a
network of the Private Industry
Councils of the cities of Carson,
Lomita and Torrance.
Membership to the program is
free and the only eligibility requirements are that participants be laid
off, unemployed or underemployed
and economically disadvantaged.
Residency in the sponsoring cities

is also a requirement.
Members will receive training in
such areas as word processing,
automotive repair and electronic
assembly and may participate in job
search workshops, skills testing and
on-the-job training.
For membership applications or
more information visit PIC works at
21818 S. Wilmington #408 or call
830-3630.

Carson News • June 1988

City Of Carson Holds
Annual Art Show

Artist Edgar Buocree displays his technique of "pointillism" 
during a demonstration at the show.

Residents attending the May 6 Artfest gaze intently at one of
the many fine pieces of work displayed.

Over 100 local artists were show-
cased in "Art Fest '88" held in the
Carson Community Center. A cham-
pagne reception started off the
annual event which featured enter-
tainment by harpist Howard Rich's
Saturday Night Bath, Johnny Blay-
lock's combo, singer Mary Nelson
and harpist Lisel Erman.

The artists displayed over 400
pieces which were judged by faculty

from Cal State Dominguez Hills, El
Camino and Harbor Colleges. The
work, which included oil, acrylic,
and watercolor painting, charcoal
sketching; sculpture and ceramics
was judged in 11 different cate-
gories with a total of 44 awards
given to outstanding artists.

Helen Bell, Assistant Coordinator
of Senior Recreation at the Com-
munity Center, estimated total vis-

itor attendance at the event to be
well over 450 people. There were
offered for sale or visitors, like stu-
dents in Carson elementary and
junior high art classes, could just
admire the work that the South Bay
artists had to offer.

"It was a great success," Bell said.
"And we expect it to be even bigger
next year."

Ibis Hotel Brings
"Taste Of France" To Carson

"French Flair Comes To Carson"
will be the theme of the grand open-
ing of the Ibis Hotel now under con-
struction in Carson.

The grand opening is scheduled
for the first two weeks in Septem-
ber, with entertainment and gala
events to be announced.

In addition to its 225 rooms, the
Ibis will have 3,000 square feet of
banquet space, with 200 person
occupancy. The hotel will also fea-

ture two restaurants for dining and
entertainment pleasure: Le Restau-
rante with authentic French cui-
sine and Claudine's Lounge with
live music and dancing.

The hotel will be close to the new
Carson Community Center which
will accommodate larger parties for
banquets and conventions. For
more information about the open-
ing or the hotel, itself, call the Ibis at
(213) 830-9200.

The Carson Report • January 1988

Trouble
Shooter

Each month this section of the news-letter will deal with a frequently asked question fielded by the staff at the City's Service Request Desk, or will highlight important new information which might be of interest to the general public.

QUESTION:
How many and what breeds of animals are city residents able to keep at their homes?

ANSWER:
The following list includes which type of animals and how many of each are allowed in a family residence.
• Cats
• Dogs
• Tropical Fish
• No more than 20 white mice
• No more than any three of the following:
Canaries        Marmo Monkeys
Chinchillas      Pigeons
Chipmunks      Rabbits
Finches          Ravens
Gopher          Monkeys
Snakes          Squirrel Monkeys
Ginney Pigs     Toucans
Hamsters        Turtles
Hawks            White Doves
King Snakes

The service request desk is a clearing house for any questions residents may have regarding the City of Carson. If you are in need of a particular service the City has to offer or would like more information regarding this issue please call 830-7600, ext. 320.

Carson
Job-Hunters
Get Lucky

Are you ready to return to the work-force but find that you don't have the technical skills? Do you just need to brush up on your typing? Or maybe you have the skills but just can't find the right opportunity?

The Job Training Center, located at 21818 Wilmington in Carson, may be the answer to your problems.

This special program is jointly spon-sored by the cities of Carson, Torrance, and Lomita, and has been in existance since 1983. Carson's fourth year of par-ticipation in the federal program, which is called the Private Industry Council, was again recognized as one of the most effective programs in the nation. The Private Industry Council's efforts in the program results in the placement of youths in summer jobs with local firms and has registered an estimated 800 youths for the program.

The Job Training Center's purpose is to teach new job skills to low-income people, dislocated workers, displaced homemakers and those with employ-ment barriers. The Center offers vocational training in the classroom in many different areas, such as elec-tronics and computer skills. They then assist participants in finding jobs that match their new job skills. By working with area companies, administrators are often able to place their graduates in well-paid jobs.

The Job Training Center points with pride to the fact that participants have a 70% employment rate after some training, at an average wage of $8.09. Enrollment in this program is lim-ited. Interested residents should call 830-3630.

City Adopts 1987–88 Fiscal Budget

The Carson City Council, after numerous public hearings and work-shops, has adopted the City's budget for fiscal year 1987–88.

Funds come from a variety of sources, including revenues from local taxes, state and federal funds, and "users fees" such as business licenses. Carson will receive additional assis-tance under new legislation passed by the State Legislature which allocates money to no- and low-tax cities such as Carson, while it shares its revenue base with the County of Los Angeles.

The total of all of Carson's budgets is $29,286,337, which includes the General Fund Operating Budget of $25,364,126. Other budgets total $4,004,211.

Carson Cleans
Up City With
Graffiti
Abatement
Program

Last summer, the Carson City Council began a new program to remove graffiti from City-owned and private property. Known as the Graffiti Abatement Program, this innovative idea allows City maintenance workers and private citizens to work together to clean up Carson.

The City's Graffiti Abatement Crew is responsible for removing graffiti from walls, fences, and buildings which are adjacent to or facing a public sidewalk or street. The City will also provide, at no charge to residents, a gallon of paint and a roller or brush so that residents may remove graffiti from their own property.

The City Crew works in seven graffiti maintenance zones throughout Carson in a sequential order, and complelets work in one zone before moving on to the next zone. You may contact Pete Mendoza at 830-7600, extension 385, to find out when the crew will be in your area, or for more information on the Graffiti Abatement Program.

The Carson 20th
Anniversary
Committee Is
Looking For
Historic Photos

As part of the City's 20th Anniver-sary activities, the Anniversary Com-mittee is planning to assemble a photo history of the City's last 20 years. A photo display of people and events important to Carson's history will be unveiled at the Anniversary Ball, Feb-ruary 27, 1988.

If you have photos of events pertain-ing to the last 20 years of the City's history and would like to lend them to the Anniversary Committee as part of the display, please contact the Public Information Office at 830-7600, ext. 315.

sections.question:
You asked the following questions about the January and 
June 1988 copies of the City of Carson monthly report entitled 
liThe Carson Report. II 
1. 
May the city publish portrait photographs of its 
elected officials in the monthly report? 
2. 
May the city publish group photographs of the city's 
elected officials in the report, if the officials are not 
identified by name? 
3. 
May the city publish a feature article introducing a 
newly appointed councilmember? 
Y, et seq. 
All references to 
regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code 
of Regulations. 
 • (916)322-5660 


William R. McKown 
August 19, 1988 
Page -2-
4. 
Is there a difference between a report being mailed at 
public expense and being delivered at public expense?
sections.conclusion:
The city may not publish photographs of or otherwise refer 
to elected officers in the city's monthly report or in any 
other mass mailing. 
This prohibition includes publishing group 
photographs in which elected city officers are not identified 
by name. 
Furthermore, for purposes of the Act a person 
appointed to a vacant elective office is an elected officer. 
Therefore, the city may not publish a feature article about a 
newly appointed councilmember. 
Nevertheless, the city may list the names of elected 
officers in a standard roster-type listing, as long as names of 
all elected officers are in the same type size and typeface. 
The Commission also has concluded that a public agency may 
not send a mass mailing in any way that results in expenditure 
of public moneys. 
Delivering a document is a form of sending a 
document. 
Therefore, the city may not spend public moneys to 
deliver mass mailings that contain the name, signature, or 
photograph of an elected officer.
sections.facts:
The city of Carson publishes a monthly report entitled "The 
Carson Report." 
On the address page of the January 1988 
report, the city published captioned portrait photographs of 
the city's seven elected officers: 
five councilmembers, the 
city clerk and city treasurer. 
The June 1988 report had several references to elected city 
officers. 
That issue's front-page feature article introduced a 
newly appointed member of the city council; the article 
included a large portrait photograph of the official. 
Inside 
the report, three different group photographs showed several 
councilmembers, who were identified by name in the captions. 
Finally, the address page had captioned portrait photographs of 
the city's seven elected officers.
sections.analysis:
California electors enacted proposition 73 in June 1988. 
Proposition 73 includes amendments to Sections 89001 and 


William R. McKown 
August 19, 1988 
Page -3-
82041.5 of the Act. 
These amendments took effect on June 8, 
1988. 
section 89001 now reads: 
89001. 
No newsletter or other mass mailing shall 
be sent at public expense. 
section 82041.5 now reads: 
82041.5. 
"Mass mailing" means two hundred or 
more substantially similar pieces of mail, but does 
not include a form letter or other mail which is sent 
in response to an unsolicited request, letter or other 
inquiry. 
These two sections could be construed to ban all 
mailings at public expense of 200 or more substantially 
similar documents. 
On July 26, 1988, however, the 
Commission adopted an emergency regulation and approved 
interim advice that allows public agencies to send certain 
mass mailings at public expense. 
(Regulation 18901; Raye 
Advice Letters, No. A-88-220, copies enclosed.) 
In the 
following paragraphs we apply emergency Regulation 18901 
and the interim advice letters to the monthly reports 
published by the city of Carson. 
Prohibition Against Spending Public Moneys To Promote Elected 
Officials 
After considering ballot arguments in favor of Proposition 
73, statements by the proponents of the proposition, and the 
language of amended section 89001, the Commission concluded 
that the intent of section 89001 is to prohibit the use of 
public moneys for the purpose of sending out mass mailings 
which assist elected officials in the quest for public office. 
(Raye Advice Letter, No. A-88-220, June 16, 1988.). 
Therefore, 
any reference to an elected officer is prohibited in a public 
agency newsletter sent to 200 or more persons in a calendar 
month, except in the standard letterhead or in a roster-type 
listing in which each elected officer's name appears in the 
same type size and typeface as the other officers. 
(See 
emergency Regulation 18901; Raye Advice Letter, No. A-88-220, 
July 1, 1988.) 
Consequently, the city may not publish portrait or group 
photographs of elected officers in the monthly report, even if 
the captions do not identify the officers by name. 
Also, 
Section 82020 defines an elected officer to include "a person 
who is appointed to fill a vacant elective office .... " 
Thus, a 


William R. McKown 
August 19, 1988 
Page -4-
newly appointed councilmember is an elected officer for 
purposes of the Act. 
Therefore, the city cannot publish and 
send a feature article about the newly appointed councilmember 
or use a photograph of the officer. 
(Also see Gonzalves Advice 
Letter, No. A-88-021, copy enclosed.) 
The city however, may 
list its elected officers in the standard letterhead or in a 
roster-type listing that uses the same type size and typeface 
for each officer. 
Sending A Mass Mailing 
For the meantime, the Commission has concluded that section 
89001 prohibits a public agency from spending public moneys to 
distribute mass mailings that contain the names or photographs 
of elected officers. 
(Raye Advice Letter, No. A-88-220, July 
1, 1988, at page 5.) 
The terms "sent" and "mailing" include 
delivering documents. (Raye Advice Letter, No. A-88-220, July 
I, 1988, at page 6, footnote 4.) 
Therefore, the city may not 
use public moneys to pay for any type of delivery service to 
distribute a mass mailing containing the name or photograph of 
an elected officer. 
I hope this letter provides satisfactory answers to your 
advice request. 
Please call me at (916) 322-5901 if you have 
any questions about this letter. 
DMG:MA:aa 
Enclosures

1. 
May the city publish portrait photographs of its

3.

Y, et seq.

 • (916)322-5660 


William R. McKown

Is there a difference between a report being mailed at

CONCLUSION: The city may not publish photographs of or otherwise refer 
to elected officers in the city's monthly report or in any 
other mass mailing. 
This prohibition includes publishing group 
photographs in which elected city officers are not identified 
by name. 
Furthermore, for purposes of the Act a person 
appointed to a vacant elective office is an elected officer. 
Therefore, the city may not publish a feature article about a 
newly appointed councilmember. 
Nevertheless, the city may list the names of elected 
officers in a standard roster-type listing, as long as names of 
all elected officers are in the same type size and typeface. 
The Commission also has concluded that a public agency may 
not send a mass mailing in any way that results in expenditure 
of public moneys. 
Delivering a document is a form of sending a 
document. 
Therefore, the city may not spend public moneys to 
deliver mass mailings that contain the name, signature, or 
photograph of an elected officer.
embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission August 19, 1988 William R. McKown Acting City Administrator City of Carson P.O. Box 6234 Carson, CA 90749 Re: Your Request For Advice Our File No. A-88-276 Dear Mr. McKown: You have requested advice about application of Section 89001 of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")1/ as amended by recently enacted Proposition 73, to the City of Carson monthly report. QUESTIONS You asked the following questions about the January and June 1988 copies of the City of Carson monthly report entitled "The Carson Report." 1. May the city publish portrait photographs of its elected officials in the monthly report? 2. May the city publish group photographs of the city's elected officials in the report, if the officials are not identified by name? 3. May the city publish a feature article introducing a newly appointed councilmember? _____________________ 1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 William R. McKown August 19, 1988 Page -2- 4. Is there a difference between a report being mailed at public expense and being delivered at public expense? CONCLUSIONS The city may not publish photographs of or otherwise refer to elected officers in the city's monthly report or in any other mass mailing. This prohibition includes publishing group photographs in which elected city officers are not identified by name. Furthermore, for purposes of the Act a person appointed to a vacant elective office is an elected officer. Therefore, the city may not publish a feature article about a newly appointed councilmember. Nevertheless, the city may list the names of elected officers in a standard roster-type listing, as long as names of all elected officers are in the same type size and typeface. The Commission also has concluded that a public agency may not send a mass mailing in any way that results in expenditure of public moneys. Delivering a document is a form of sending a document. Therefore, the city may not spend public moneys to deliver mass mailings that contain the name, signature, or photograph of an elected officer. FACTS The city of Carson publishes a monthly report entitled "The Carson Report." On the address page of the January 1988 report, the city published captioned portrait photographs of the city's seven elected officers: five councilmembers, the city clerk and city treasurer. The June 1988 report had several references to elected city officers. That issue's front-page feature article introduced a newly appointed member of the city council; the article included a large portrait photograph of the official. Inside the report, three different group photographs showed several councilmembers, who were identified by name in the captions. Finally, the address page had captioned portrait photographs of the city's seven elected officers.
